resources.string: Veritas
%1$s is one signal Veritas records during a scan. The detail depends on the media type and the measurements attached to this result.
%1$s matters only in context. Veritas weighs it with the other detector and provenance signals before choosing a verdict.
This signal is not proof by itself. Capture conditions, editing, platform recompression, or unusual source material can create similar measurements in authentic media.
SPATIAL
Generative image pattern
The image model found spatial and frequency patterns that are more common in generated imagery than in camera captures.
This is the strongest image signal because it comes from the detector trained on real and synthetic image examples. It is still interpreted with metadata and quality checks.
Highly edited images, screenshots, low-quality reposts, and aggressive compression can make authentic images resemble generated samples.
EXIF
Missing camera metadata
No camera make, model, or capture metadata was found in the image file.
Real camera files often carry metadata, while generated images and platform exports often do not. Veritas treats this as supporting evidence only.
Many authentic screenshots, social uploads, and privacy-preserving apps remove EXIF metadata.
METADATA
Suspicious image encoding
JPEG quantization or image metadata does not match common camera or editor export patterns.
Encoding fingerprints can reveal when an image was synthesized, re-saved, or assembled through an unusual toolchain.
Editing apps, memes, screenshots, and platform recompression can also produce unusual encoding fingerprints.
ELA
Error-level anomaly
Error-level analysis found regions whose compression behavior differs from the surrounding image.
Local compression differences can point to editing, compositing, or generated regions that were introduced after the original image was encoded.
Multiple saves, filters, stickers, and normal edits can create similar ELA differences in authentic images.
VOICE
Synthetic voice pattern
The audio model found speech features that resemble neural voice generation or voice conversion.
Voice-clone detectors are strongest when speech is clear and long enough to expose spectral and timing artifacts.
Noisy recordings, speakerphone audio, codec damage, and accented or unusual speech can reduce reliability.
CODEC
Audio codec mismatch
The audio compression profile is less plausible for the file type and recording path.
Synthetic clips are often exported, transcoded, or inserted into containers in ways that do not match natural phone recordings.
Messaging apps, editors, and screen recordings can transcode authentic audio into unusual profiles.
SHORT
Audio too short
The clip does not contain enough speech for a reliable detector read.
Short clips may not expose the spectral, timing, and prosody patterns needed to separate human and synthetic speech.
This is a confidence warning, not a synthetic signal. A short authentic clip can trigger it.
VOICE OK
Natural speech signal
The audio model did not find strong synthetic speech artifacts in this clip.
A clean speech signal lowers synthetic confidence when it agrees with codec and quality checks.
High-quality voice clones or heavily transformed audio can still evade this detector.
FRAMES
Synthetic-looking frames
One or more sampled frames contain image-level patterns associated with generated visuals.
Frame evidence helps localize where the detector saw suspicious content, especially when only part of a clip is synthetic.
Static-image evidence does not prove a temporal deepfake. Recompression, overlays, and stylized footage can trigger similar frame-level signals.
DRIFT
Temporal drift
The video model saw frame-to-frame embedding changes that were less consistent than expected for natural motion.
Temporal consistency can expose generation artifacts that are not visible in a single still frame.
Fast cuts, screen recordings, stabilization, and low frame rates can also create abrupt temporal changes.
FACE
Face consistency issue
Face-region analysis found a consistency score outside the expected range for the sampled frames.
Face regions are high-value forensic areas because many manipulations concentrate around eyes, mouth, skin, and facial boundaries.
Small faces, motion blur, masks, makeup, occlusion, or unusual lighting can make face-region analysis less reliable.
DECODE
Video decode failed
The video could not be decoded far enough for detector inference.
Decoder failures explain why Veritas cannot make a confident visual or temporal claim about the clip.
This is not evidence of manipulation. Corrupt downloads, unsupported encoders, or damaged files can cause the same result.
CODEC OK
Capture chain looks consistent
The media did not show strong codec or model signs of synthetic generation in this scan.
A clean negative signal helps avoid over-calling synthetic content when the detector evidence is weak.
Absence of detected artifacts is not proof of authenticity, especially for high-quality generated media or heavily transformed reposts.
QUALITY
Quality-limited evidence
The scan was affected by low resolution, short duration, heavy compression, missing frames, or a runtime fallback.
Quality limits are surfaced because they explain why Veritas may return an uncertain verdict instead of forcing a binary answer.
Quality warnings often come from ordinary platform recompression or old files. They reduce confidence rather than proving manipulation.
C2PA OK
Signed Content Credentials
The file contains a valid signed provenance manifest from a known issuer.
A valid signature can strongly support authenticity because it ties the file to a capture or editing chain.
A valid manifest describes provenance, not absolute truth. It may not cover later screen recordings or copies.
C2PA BAD
Invalid provenance signature
The file claims Content Credentials, but the signature does not validate.
A forged or altered manifest is stronger tampering evidence than a file with no manifest at all.
File corruption or broken export tools can also damage a manifest, so Veritas weighs this with other evidence.
C2PA REV
Revoked signing authority
The signing authority for this file has been revoked since signing.
Revocation means Veritas can no longer treat the signature as a clean authenticity signal.
Revocation can happen for administrative reasons unrelated to this file.
SYNTHID
AI watermark detected
A known generator watermark signature was detected in the media.
Watermarks are direct provenance evidence when they survive export and recompression.
Watermark availability depends on generator support and may be affected by cropping, recompression, or edits.
FREQ
Diffusion spectral signature
Frequency-domain structure resembles artifacts left by diffusion-style image generation.
Generated images can leave regular patterns that real camera optics and sensors usually do not create.
Scaling, sharpening, screenshots, and platform recompression can create misleading frequency patterns.
ELA
Inconsistent compression region
A localized region compresses differently from the surrounding media.
This can indicate a generated or edited region inserted into an otherwise different image.
Normal editing, stickers, text overlays, and repeated exports can trigger this signal.
METADATA
Implausible metadata
Embedded metadata does not match the media properties or expected capture path.
Metadata mismatches can reveal tampering, synthetic export, or an inconsistent provenance chain.
Some authentic files have inconsistent metadata after editing, sharing, or platform processing.
